caveats: a: O*NET describes occupation tasks and importance, not industry payroll shares or time spent.
a: The industry also employs assistants, accountants, marketers, and executives whose mix is not separately weighted here.
a: Exposure means technical opportunity, not that clients or counterparties will accept autonomous execution.
rho: The Census adoption figures cover all nonfarm sectors and any business function, not this industry or labor substitution.
rho: Small agencies may adopt general-purpose tools quickly while still failing to redesign workflows enough to realize labor savings.
rho: Client consent, privacy, union rules, and unauthorized-practice concerns can constrain deployment in sensitive advice and negotiation.
e: The supplied n is an estimated EBITDA-band count and may include firms with materially different agency, management, and business-management models.
e: Occupation self-employment is not the same population as LMM employer firms.
e: Eligibility can change abruptly when a star client or rainmaker departs.
s5: The survey is voluntary, cross-industry, and oriented toward owners engaged with exit planning.
s5: Exit intent is not a completed qualifying control transfer.
s5: Public deal databases can undercount small private transactions.
q: SAG-AFTRA rules apply to covered performers and franchised agents, not all sports, literary, music, influencer, or public-figure representation.
q: Commission economics can retain cost savings but also make revenue volatile with client earnings.
q: The estimate excludes demand or volume loss, which is reflected in d5 and o.
d5: Employment is not service quantity and includes self-employed workers.
d5: The ten-year national occupation projection is converted to a five-year industry demand ratio.
d5: The source does not isolate the LMM agency population or constant-quality service basket.
o: California licensing and SAG-AFTRA franchise rules cover important but incomplete slices of the national industry.
o: Sports, literary, music, influencer, and business-management segments have different operator requirements.
o: Technology may unbundle low-value clients from full-service representation faster than it replaces high-stakes representation.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Agents and Managers for Artists, Athletes, Entertainers, and Other Public Figures

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The represented person is the client; revenue is predominantly a contractual percentage of client earnings, bookings, endorsements, or other commissionable income, with some recurring management or business-management fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Solo practitioners without transferable client relationships or operating infrastructure, captive representation units, one-off event promoters, law firms acting only as legal counsel, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US agencies and management firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly represent and manage artists, performers, athletes, creators, and other public figures for commissions or recurring management fees.

caveats: O*NET describes occupation tasks and importance, not industry payroll shares or time spent.
The industry also employs assistants, accountants, marketers, and executives whose mix is not separately weighted here.
Exposure means technical opportunity, not that clients or counterparties will accept autonomous execution.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across independent talent, literary, music, creator, and sports agencies that reports labor hours and payroll by workflow, plus before-and-after staffing data from agencies deploying AI-enabled CRM, contract-review, and booking tools.
a.rationale: A task-by-task judgment from O*NET assigns high exposure to information gathering, promotional-material drafting and targeting, scheduling, meeting coordination, travel and business administration, payment monitoring, and periodic accounting. Negotiation, relationship development, talent evaluation, career strategy, and high-stakes advice are only partly substitutable. The result is an estimated wage-weighted share because no source measures time or wages by task for this six-digit industry.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in the core occupation from 21,400 in 2024 to 23,200 in 2034, an 8.7% ten-year increase. The base annualizes that change and applies five years, producing about 1.04. Occupational employment is a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service demand: productivity gains could make it understate demand, while occupational migration, self-employment estimation, or changing service intensity could make it overstate demand.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity and includes self-employed workers.
The ten-year national occupation projection is converted to a five-year industry demand ratio.
The source does not isolate the LMM agency population or constant-quality service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar revenue, active-client, booking, endorsement, and contract-negotiation volumes for 711410 from Census Service Annual Survey microdata or a representative panel of agencies, separated from commission-rate and client-income inflation.
d5.rationale: The official projection implies modest positive demand for representation and management labor rather than collapse. The wider range allows weaker entertainment/sports monetization, client self-service, or stronger creator-market growth than the employment projection embeds.

caveats: The supplied n is an estimated EBITDA-band count and may include firms with materially different agency, management, and business-management models.
Occupation self-employment is not the same population as LMM employer firms.
Eligibility can change abruptly when a star client or rainmaker departs.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence on the 139 supplied LMM candidates covering top-client concentration, client contract assignability and termination rights, producer-versus-firm origination, second-layer management, recurring revenue, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: Most firms in the code serve external clients through repeat representation and management, but eligibility is reduced by founder-centric books, extreme dependence on one or two star clients, and operations whose relationships cannot survive a change of control. BLS reports 26.1% self-employment in the core occupation, reinforcing the prevalence of individual-practitioner economics, although the supplied LMM firm count is an employer-firm construct.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 711410 ($4,264,386,690) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 711410 ($11,250,051,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $458,288 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 711410); class <5: 3,969 firms, avg revenue/firm $670,165 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $111,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 413 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,564,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $426,721 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 182 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,084,995 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $846,143 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,354,967 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,222,267 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,940,706 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,807,733 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $283,878,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $47,237,299 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 139

caveats: California licensing and SAG-AFTRA franchise rules cover important but incomplete slices of the national industry.
Sports, literary, music, influencer, and business-management segments have different operator requirements.
Technology may unbundle low-value clients from full-service representation faster than it replaces high-stakes representation.
o.change_evidence: Representative client-level data on direct versus agent-mediated bookings and renewals, platform-originated deal share, contract outcomes, client churn after dropping representation, and regulatory or union changes permitting software-only procurement and negotiation.
o.rationale: Software can let clients self-serve discovery, outreach, scheduling, analytics, and routine administration, but accountable operators retain roles in access, reputation, conflict handling, fiduciary judgment, negotiation, and regulated procurement. California law defines talent agencies around procuring employment, and SAG-AFTRA requires members in most cities to use franchised agents for covered securing and negotiation work; managers also supply personalized career direction beyond software output.

caveats: SAG-AFTRA rules apply to covered performers and franchised agents, not all sports, literary, music, influencer, or public-figure representation.
Commission economics can retain cost savings but also make revenue volatile with client earnings.
The estimate excludes demand or volume loss, which is reflected in d5 and o.
q.change_evidence: A cohort of agency and management contracts and five-year renewals showing commission changes, service-scope expansion, agent compensation, client churn, and realized margin after AI deployment, segmented by union status and representation vertical.
q.rationale: Commission contracts tie agency revenue to client earnings rather than billable labor, so internal productivity gains can initially be retained. SAG-AFTRA says franchised agents may charge no more than 10% and only earn commission when the performer is paid; managers generally charge 10%-15%. Over renewals, competitive commission pressure, client demands for more service, producer compensation, and investment in client acquisition share part of the benefit.

caveats: The Census adoption figures cover all nonfarm sectors and any business function, not this industry or labor substitution.
Small agencies may adopt general-purpose tools quickly while still failing to redesign workflows enough to realize labor savings.
Client consent, privacy, union rules, and unauthorized-practice concerns can constrain deployment in sensitive advice and negotiation.
rho.change_evidence: A 2027-2031 longitudinal survey of 711410 firms measuring production use by workflow, exception rates, quality incidents, system integration, labor hours avoided, and headcount avoided, segmented by firm size and representation vertical.
rho.rationale: Digital, text-heavy administrative workflows make implementation plausible within five years, but relationship ownership, client confidentiality, legal review, fragmented agency systems, and the need for human approval in negotiation limit conversion. Census found only 17%-20% of US businesses using AI in late 2025 through early May 2026, with less than 20% usage among firms with four or fewer employees, indicating a material adoption gap even before workflow depth is considered.

s5.bridge: The source population is 1,162 US business-owner respondents across industries, 49% of whom said they wanted to exit within five years. This is transformed downward to a qualifying-control-transfer probability because intentions are not completed transfers, 70% of respondents preferred internal options, and key-person agencies may be nontransferable. Selection into an exit-planning survey likely biases stated intent upward; aging and unsolicited sales can offset some downward adjustment.
caveats: The survey is voluntary, cross-industry, and oriented toward owners engaged with exit planning.
Exit intent is not a completed qualifying control transfer.
Public deal databases can undercount small private transactions.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, or GF Data transaction counts for US 711410 targets matched to the supplied 139-firm denominator, including add-ons and founder recapitalizations but excluding minority financings and internal reorganizations; owner-age and succession-plan data for the same firms.
s5.rationale: A meaningful five-year transfer pool is plausible, but a control sale to an outside operator is only one exit route. The range discounts broad owner exit intent for internal succession, nonexecution, closures, and agency-specific client portability risk.

principal_driver: Commission-based revenue can retain AI-enabled labor savings while human agents continue to own access, negotiation, and accountability.
principal_weakness: Client and rainmaker relationships may walk at acquisition, making apparent agency earnings far less transferable than the firm count suggests.
